title: Mutation and aliases

publication.changeSummary: Topic-specific draft for Mutation and aliases; requires human source, technical, pedagogy, and accessibility review. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: CS2023 Software Development Fundamentals; August 2023; section applicable to Mutation and aliases
sources.claim: Rebinding changes which value a name denotes, while mutation changes the state of an existing mutable object that may be reachable through more than one name.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Rebinding changes which value a name denotes, while mutation changes the state of an existing mutable object that may be reachable through more than one name.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: Two names refer to the same shopping-list object; appending through one name makes the added item visible when reading through the other. Your goal is to predict visible changes when two names refer to the same mutable collection. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Pseudocode; Variables and assignment. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Operational model
sections.body: Rebinding changes which value a name denotes, while mutation changes the state of an existing mutable object that may be reachable through more than one name. The learner should use this model to predict an observable result rather than merely repeat terminology.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure point
sections.body: Languages differ in reference, value, copy, and immutability semantics, so alias behavior must be confirmed for the concrete type and operation. A correct explanation therefore includes the applicable assumptions and avoids this common mistake: Assigning or updating through one name can never affect a value observed through another name.

includes: Recognize the conceptual difference between rebinding a name and changing a shared value.
Predict visible changes when two names refer to the same mutable collection.
excludes: Languages differ in reference, value, copy, and immutability semantics, so alias behavior must be confirmed for the concrete type and operation.

outcomes: Predict visible changes when two names refer to the same mutable collection.

examples.title: Mutation and aliases: direct case
examples.situation: Two names refer to the same shopping-list object; appending through one name makes the added item visible when reading through the other.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the starting evidence: Two names refer to the same shopping-list object; appending through one name makes the added item visible when reading through the other.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Check the result against this correction: First trace which names reach the same mutable object, then distinguish changing that object from rebinding one name to a different value.
examples.result: The case supports the declared outcome because First trace which names reach the same mutable object, then distinguish changing that object from rebinding one name to a different value.

examples.title: Mutation and aliases: boundary case
examples.situation: Reassigning one name to a new list leaves the original shared list unchanged for another name that still refers to it.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed context: Reassigning one name to a new list leaves the original shared list unchanged for another name that still refers to it.
Step 2 — Keep this boundary explicit: Languages differ in reference, value, copy, and immutability semantics, so alias behavior must be confirmed for the concrete type and operation.
Step 3 — Reject the documented misconception: Assigning or updating through one name can never affect a value observed through another name.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Predict visible changes when two names refer to the same mutable collection.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Assigning or updating through one name can never affect a value observed through another name.
misconceptions.correction: First trace which names reach the same mutable object, then distinguish changing that object from rebinding one name to a different value.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using the direct and boundary scenarios, explain why this belief fails: Assigning or updating through one name can never affect a value observed through another name.

analogy.example: A labeled scoreboard can model Mutation and aliases: each label exposes a current value and each recorded event produces the next visible state.
usefulFor: Reasoning about values, expressions, named state, and changes across time
limitations: The scoreboard does not accurately model binary representation, scope, aliases, object identity, or language coercion.
Languages differ in reference, value, copy, and immutability semantics, so alias behavior must be confirmed for the concrete type and operation.

scaffold: Line 2: state the part of the core rule that applies.
Line 3: give the result and reject the common mistake.
expectedEvidence: Named example: Mutation and aliases: direct case.

recap.summary: You should now be able to predict visible changes when two names refer to the same mutable collection. Keep this boundary in view: Languages differ in reference, value, copy, and immutability semantics, so alias behavior must be confirmed for the concrete type and operation.
retrievalPrompts: Explain Mutation and aliases without using the optional analogy and include one observable consequence.
Why is this statement incorrect: Assigning or updating through one name can never affect a value observed through another name.
Apply the model to this case from memory: Reassigning one name to a new list leaves the original shared list unchanged for another name that still refers to it.
recap.nextReviewPrompt: Next: continue to “Sequence”. Before moving on, answer one retrieval prompt without reopening the explanation.
